summary: DeVault argues that simplicity should be the highest priority in software development, above correctness and performance. He contends that complex software inevitably breaks and becomes slow, while simple software is easier to debug, profile, and maintain. When problems arise at 2 AM, simple code is what saves you. He advocates ruthlessly reducing scope to find basic problems that can be solved with basic solutions, then composing those simple solutions to address complexity.
key_points: Simplicity is the most important quality in software because without it, correctness and performance are unattainable
Simple software is easier to test, debug, and profile when inevitable bugs and performance problems surface
Complex software becomes a liability during maintenance — when workloads increase or requirements change
Developers should ruthlessly remove scope to decompose problems into basic components with basic solutions
Complex problems are better served by composing simple solutions than by applying complex solutions directly
money_quotes: The single most important quality in a piece of software is simplicity.
If your solution is not simple, it will not be correct or fast.
Given enough time, you'll find that all software which solves sufficiently complex problems is going to (1) have bugs and (2) have performance problems.
The only way to find out for sure what's slow is to measure.

When you're stressed out at 2 AM on Tuesday morning because the server shat itself because your 1,831st new customer pushed the billing system over the edge, do you think you're well equipped to find the problem in a complex piece of code you last saw a year ago?
You need to take problems apart, identify smaller problems within them and ruthlessly remove scope until you find the basic problem you can apply a basic solution to.
The complex problem comes later, and it'll be better served by the composition of simple solutions than with the application of a complex solution.
key_insight: Simplicity is not a luxury but a prerequisite — without it, software cannot be made correct or fast, and complex problems are best solved by composing simple solutions rather than building complex ones.
